heroOverview: Fibrothorax (ICD-10: J94.1) is a condition in which dense fibrous scar tissue fuses the two pleural layers — the visceral pleura covering the lung and the parietal pleura lining the chest wall — effectively encasing the lung in a rigid fibrous shell that prevents it from fully expanding with each breath. The scarring is almost always the end result of a preceding pleural space injury: an incompletely drained haemothorax or empyema are the two most common triggers, each capable of depositing fibrin that organises over weeks to months into thick collagen-rich 'peel' across the entire pleural surface. The resulting restrictive ventilatory defect reduces the lung's volume on the affected side, causes progressive breathlessness, and, in severe bilateral cases, can impair gas exchange and produce respiratory failure. When one or both lungs are fully entrapped, surgical decortication — stripping the fibrous peel from the lung surface — is the only intervention that can restore meaningful lung expansion. Early and complete drainage of haemothorax and pleural empyema remains the most effective strategy for preventing fibrothorax from forming in the first place.

keyStats.prevalence: Fibrothorax complicates an estimated 1-2% of undrained or partially drained haemothoraces and 10-30% of complicated parapneumonic effusions (pleural empyema) that are managed without timely drainage
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults with prior pleural infection, haemothorax from trauma, or thoracic surgery complications; rare in children except following haemothorax or purulent pleurisy
keyStats.avgAge: Reflects the age distribution of the causative conditions; most cases occur in adults aged 30-70 years with prior chest trauma, thoracic infection, or surgery
keyStats.globalCases: Exact worldwide incidence is not tracked; estimated tens of thousands of cases annually given the global burden of pleural empyema and traumatic haemothorax

definition: Fibrothorax (ICD-10 J94.1) refers to the formation of thick fibrous scar tissue within the pleural space, binding the visceral pleura of the lung to the parietal pleura of the chest wall and progressively restricting lung expansion. The pleural space normally contains only a thin film of lubricating fluid that allows the two pleural layers to slide freely. When this space is invaded by blood, pus, or persistent inflammatory exudate, the fibrin networks that form as part of normal repair are not reabsorbed but instead become populated by fibroblasts and then organised into dense collagen sheets — a process known as pleural organisation. The resulting fibrous 'peel' or 'cortex' can be anywhere from a few millimetres to more than a centimetre thick, covering part or all of the pleural surface on one or both sides. Physiologically, the fixed chest wall and trapped lung create a restrictive ventilatory pattern: total lung capacity, vital capacity, and functional residual capacity all fall on the affected side. In unilateral fibrothorax, mediastinal shift toward the affected side may occur, compressing the contralateral lung. In severe bilateral disease, respiratory failure supervenes. Fibrothorax is classified under ICD-10 category J94 (Other pleural conditions) and managed by thoracic surgeons and pulmonologists. The pathology is identical regardless of the initial trigger — haemothorax, empyema, tuberculosis pleurisy, asbestos exposure, or radiotherapy to the chest — differing only in the chronology and radiographic appearance of the pleural opacity. Early and aggressive drainage of the precipitating pleural collection before fibrin organisation sets in is the most effective form of secondary prevention.

typesClassification.description: Follows undrained or incompletely drained blood in the pleural space from trauma, surgery, or spontaneous haemothorax. Blood that remains for more than 7-10 days undergoes fibrin deposition and subsequently organises into thick pleural peel.

The most common cause globally; arises from a complicated parapneumonic effusion or primary pleural empyema where the fibrinopurulent exudate is not drained in the acute phase, leading to a thick organising cortex over weeks to months.

A significant cause in regions where tuberculosis is endemic; Mycobacterium tuberculosis pleurisy tends to produce a particularly dense bilateral pleural reaction and is associated with calcified pleural plaques on chest imaging.

Complicates chest irradiation for lung, breast, oesophageal, or lymphomatous malignancies; radiation-induced pleural fibrosis typically appears years to decades after treatment and may co-exist with radiation pneumonitis.
Diffuse pleural thickening and fibrothorax from asbestos exposure, characterised by hyaline pleural plaques and, in severe cases, circumferential encasement of both lungs.

primarySymptoms: Progressive breathlessness on exertion, often the dominant complaint, resulting from the restricted lung expansion on the affected side as fibrous tissue prevents the lung from fully inflating with each breath.
Reduced exercise tolerance — initially noticed on climbing stairs or carrying loads, advancing over months to dyspnoea at minimal activity in severe or bilateral fibrothorax.
A dull, non-pleuritic chest ache or heaviness on the affected side, distinct from the sharp pleuritic pain of the acute pleural infection that preceded the fibrothorax.
Chest asymmetry — the affected hemithorax appears visibly smaller and moves less with breathing as the entrapped lung causes progressive volume loss.
Paradoxical or reduced chest wall movement on the affected side during deep inspiration, detectable on careful examination and accentuated during forced breathing.
A persistent dry cough in some patients, attributed to compression of the underlying lung parenchyma and mediastinal shift, although cough is less prominent than breathlessness.
Recurrent respiratory infections or pneumonia on the affected side, because the atelectatic, poorly-ventilated entrapped lung is prone to retained secretions and bacterial colonisation.
Mediastinal shift symptoms in severe unilateral cases — tracheal deviation toward the affected side and, rarely, cardiac positional change producing palpitations or haemodynamic effects.
Fatigue and reduced stamina from the chronic mechanical work of breathing against a stiff pleural envelope, compounded by any hypoxaemia if gas exchange is impaired.
Pleuritic chest pain during intercurrent respiratory infections superimposed on established fibrothorax, where the thickened pleura limits the compensatory breathing response.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent chest X-ray opacity or pleural thickening that does not resolve over six to eight weeks after treatment of the initial haemothorax or empyema.
A slowly progressive reduction in exercise capacity over months after apparent resolution of the acute pleural illness.
Chest wall asymmetry on examination or a persistently dull percussion note over the affected side beyond the expected recovery period.
Worsening dyspnoea on spirometry follow-up, showing a gradually declining forced vital capacity.
emergencySigns: Severe breathlessness at rest or oxygen saturation below 90% on pulse oximetry, indicating acute respiratory failure requiring immediate hospital assessment.
High fever with purulent sputum or new haemoptysis in a patient with fibrothorax, suggesting re-infection or secondary empyema within the scarred pleural space.
Sudden deterioration with severe chest pain and increasing hypoxaemia, which may indicate a pneumothorax or haemothorax in the already compromised pleural space.
Confusion, altered consciousness, or cyanosis in bilateral fibrothorax, signalling critically impaired gas exchange.

causes.description: Blood in the pleural space after chest trauma, surgery, or spontaneous bleeding undergoes fibrin deposition within 24-48 hours. If not evacuated within approximately 7-10 days, fibroblast invasion transforms the fibrin lattice into organised connective tissue that progressively thickens into pleural peel over four to twelve weeks.

Bacterial pneumonia can spread to the pleural space, producing an exudative effusion that progresses through fibrinopurulent and organising stages. Without timely drainage, fibrin strands in the fibrinopurulent stage become organised into thick pleural cortex — this is the single most common cause of fibrothorax worldwide.

Mycobacterium tuberculosis pleurisy stimulates an intense granulomatous pleural inflammatory reaction that, in the absence of effective treatment, resolves with particularly dense bilateral calcified pleural fibrosis. In countries with high TB prevalence, tuberculosis is a leading cause of fibrothorax.

Cumulative asbestos fibre deposition on the parietal pleura triggers chronic mesothelial irritation and fibroblast activation, producing diffuse pleural thickening and, in heavily exposed individuals, circumferential fibrothorax encasing both lungs over years to decades.

Radiation therapy to the thorax for lung, breast, oesophageal, or mediastinal cancers damages the pleural mesothelium and underlying microvasculature, provoking a late-onset fibrosing pleuritis. Fibrothorax from radiation may not become clinically apparent for years after treatment.

causes.cause: Chronic or recurrent pleural effusion from other causes
causes.description: Persistent exudative effusions from rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, or post-cardiac injury syndrome can deposit enough fibrin over months to years to produce significant pleural thickening and occasionally frank fibrothorax, particularly when treated inadequately.

riskFactors.factor: Delayed or incomplete drainage of haemothorax
riskFactors.description: Every 24 hours that clotted blood remains in the pleural space increases the probability of fibrin organisation. Timely thoracostomy drainage within the first week and video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) for retained haemothorax within 3-5 days markedly reduces fibrothorax risk.

Empyemas managed with inadequate drainage, delayed surgical intervention, or shortened antibiotic courses more frequently progress to the organising phase and dense cortex formation. Early VATS or open drainage during the fibrinopurulent phase dramatically reduces fibrothorax incidence.

Tuberculosis pleurisy carries a higher intrinsic risk of organising into fibrothorax than pyogenic empyema, related to the granulomatous nature of the inflammation. Effective anti-TB treatment reduces but does not eliminate this risk, particularly in late-presenting cases.

Cumulative asbestos fibre burden (measured in fibre-years) correlates with the severity of pleural thickening and the risk of bilateral diffuse fibrothorax. The latency from first exposure to clinically significant fibrothorax can be 20-40 years.

Post-operative pleural bleeding, air leaks, and infection after thoracic operations increase the likelihood of pleural organisation. Post-cardiac surgery pleural reactions (Dressler syndrome) and post-pleurodesis procedures can also organise into fibrothorax.

Thoracic irradiation doses above approximately 40 Gy are associated with a measurable incidence of late radiation-induced pleural fibrosis. Modern conformal and intensity-modulated radiotherapy techniques reduce but do not eliminate this risk.

Immunocompromised patients — those with HIV, haematological malignancy, or on long-term immunosuppressive therapy — are at higher risk for pleural infections that are more likely to progress to organised empyema and fibrothorax due to impaired fibrinolytic and inflammatory resolution.

affectedDemographics: Adults with prior thoracic trauma resulting in haemothorax, particularly those treated by small-bore intercostal drain or aspiration rather than early surgical drainage
Patients recovering from bacterial pneumonia with a parapneumonic effusion that was not drained or was drained late in its course
Persons with a history of pleural tuberculosis, especially in endemic regions of Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and Eastern Europe
Occupationally asbestos-exposed individuals — shipbuilders, insulators, boilermakers, and construction workers with decades of asbestos contact
Cancer survivors who have received chest radiotherapy, including breast, lung, oesophageal, and mediastinal lymphoma treatments
Patients with connective tissue diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus) that cause recurrent pleural effusions
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of fibrothorax is primarily imaging-based and confirmed by physiological testing. The history provides the first clue: a patient with prior haemothorax, empyema, tuberculosis, asbestos exposure, or chest irradiation who develops progressive exertional dyspnoea and reduced chest wall movement on one side should prompt chest imaging. A plain chest X-ray typically shows a homogeneous pleural opacity, reduced hemithoracic volume, and often mediastinal shift toward the affected side; calcified pleural plaques are seen in tuberculous and asbestos-related fibrothorax. High-resolution CT of the chest is the definitive imaging study — it delineates the thickness of the pleural cortex, its extent, and any underlying lung parenchymal damage; a cortex thicker than 2 cm covering more than half the pleural surface is generally accepted as the threshold for symptomatic restriction and surgical candidacy. Pulmonary function tests demonstrate a restrictive pattern — reduced total lung capacity, vital capacity, and functional residual capacity — with a preserved or elevated FEV1/FVC ratio, distinguishing fibrothorax from the obstructive pattern of COPD. The degree of restriction on PFTs, combined with CT cortex thickness, guides the surgical decision. In ambiguous cases where coexisting parenchymal disease is suspected, CT ventilation-perfusion imaging or a split-function nuclear scan can estimate how much of the restriction is from pleural versus parenchymal disease — critical information before decortication, since a fibrotic underlying lung will not expand after peel removal and surgery would provide no benefit. Thoracocentesis or pleural biopsy may be needed if the aetiology is uncertain, particularly to exclude a malignant pleural tumour such as mesothelioma presenting with progressive pleural thickening.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to detect pleural opacity, reduced hemithoracic volume, mediastinal shift, and calcified plaques that suggest the underlying aetiology (tuberculous or asbestos-related).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standing two-view chest X-ray takes approximately two minutes. You hold your breath briefly while the image is captured. Results are usually reviewed within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive imaging that measures pleural cortex thickness and extent, identifies the underlying lung, quantifies volume loss, distinguishes fibrothorax from malignant pleural disease, and guides surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a CT scanner for 5-10 minutes. An iodinated contrast injection through a vein may be given to enhance soft-tissue detail. The radiologist measures cortex thickness on the resulting cross-sectional images.

diagnosticTests.test: Pulmonary function tests (spirometry and full lung volumes)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the pattern and severity of ventilatory restriction — reduced TLC, VC, and FRC with preserved FEV1/FVC — and provides a baseline against which post-operative improvement can be measured.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe in and out through a mouthpiece attached to a spirometer; the technician guides you through maximal effort manoeuvres. Lung volume measurements take about 30 minutes total.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures blood oxygen and carbon dioxide levels to identify hypoxaemia or hypercapnia in more severe bilateral cases, informing the urgency of intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample is taken from an artery in the wrist (radial artery); the site is numbed with local anaesthetic first. The sample is analysed within minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Estimates the contribution of each lung to total function; confirms that the trapped lung retains viable parenchyma that will expand after surgical decortication, avoiding futile surgery when the underlying lung is irreversibly damaged.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You inhale a radioactive gas and receive a small intravenous injection of labelled particles; a gamma camera images the lungs in both projections. The entire study takes about 60-90 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains tissue or fluid for histology, culture, and cytology when the aetiology of pleural thickening is uncertain, particularly to exclude mesothelioma or tuberculosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under local anaesthetic and ultrasound guidance, a needle or small cutting device is passed between the ribs to sample the pleural tissue or fluid; the procedure takes 15-30 minutes in a procedure room.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies residual free fluid or loculations around the fibrothorax, guides drainage of any accessible collections, and assesses diaphragmatic mobility.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is placed on the chest wall with ultrasound gel; the scan is painless and takes about 15 minutes. Breathing instructions are given during the scan.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of established fibrothorax is determined by the severity of restriction, the patient's symptoms, underlying lung viability, and surgical fitness. In mild cases where restriction is limited and breathlessness manageable, a conservative approach of pulmonary rehabilitation, physiotherapy, and monitoring is appropriate. For patients with significant dyspnoea and proven viable underlying lung on functional imaging, surgical decortication is the definitive treatment. Decortication involves surgically peeling the fibrous cortex from the visceral pleural surface of the lung to allow full re-expansion, and from the parietal surface of the chest wall to restore its mobility. The operation may be performed by video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) in early-stage or thinner fibrothorax, or by open thoracotomy (the Fowler-Stephenson decortication) for dense, calcified, or extensively organised cortex. VATS decortication offers shorter hospital stay, less post-operative pain, and faster recovery, but technical limitations in thick cortex often require conversion to open surgery. Intrapleural fibrinolytic therapy — instilling tissue plasminogen activator and DNase into the pleural space — is used in the earlier fibrinopurulent stage of empyema to prevent progression to organised fibrothorax, not to treat established fibrothorax itself. Medical management includes optimising the underlying lung disease (bronchodilators for any coexisting airflow obstruction), treating any superimposed infection promptly, and providing supplemental oxygen for significant hypoxaemia. In patients unfit for surgery — usually because of severely limited cardiopulmonary reserve, bilateral disease, or non-expandable underlying lung — palliative management with oxygen, opioids for breathlessness, and pulmonary rehabilitation offers the best available symptomatic relief. Prevention remains paramount: thoracic guidelines recommend early drainage of all haemothoraces and empyemas, with VATS intervention for retained haemothorax within 3-5 days and for fibrinopurulent empyema before cortex formation is complete.

medicalTreatments.name: Intrapleural fibrinolytic therapy (tPA/DNase)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in the fibrinopurulent stage of empyema — before fibrothorax is established — to dissolve fibrin septations and facilitate drainage, reducing the likelihood of progression to organised cortex. The MIST2 trial regimen instils intrapleural tissue plasminogen activator 10 mg and DNase 5 mg via a chest drain twice daily for three days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The MIST2 trial showed a significant reduction in surgical referral rate and pleural opacity at three months compared with placebo in fibrinopurulent empyema, with surgery avoided in approximately 35% more patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and breathing exercises
medicalTreatments.description: Incentive spirometry, directed coughing, and active cycle of breathing techniques improve secretion clearance from the atelectatic lung, maintain chest wall mobility, and reduce the risk of post-operative complications. Continued long-term after surgery to maximise the functional benefit of decortication.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pre-operative respiratory physiotherapy reduces post-operative pulmonary complications by approximately 50% in thoracic surgery patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Bronchodilator therapy for coexisting airflow obstruction
medicalTreatments.description: Some patients with fibrothorax also have coexisting COPD or asthma; inhaled long-acting bronchodilators (LAMA and/or LABA) improve exercise tolerance and reduce dynamic hyperinflation in these individuals, even though they do not affect the pleural pathology itself.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LABA/LAMA combination improves FEV1 by approximately 100-200 mL and reduces breathlessness scores by a clinically meaningful margin in COPD-spectrum disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-term supplemental oxygen therapy
medicalTreatments.description: For patients with fibrothorax and resting PaO2 ≤ 7.3 kPa (or ≤ 8.0 kPa with cor pulmonale or polycythaemia), long-term oxygen therapy at 15 hours per day reduces mortality and improves quality of life, as in COPD-related hypoxaemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term oxygen therapy improves 5-year survival by approximately 50% in patients with COPD-equivalent hypoxaemia; extrapolated benefit applies to fibrothorax-related hypoxaemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of underlying cause (antibiotics for empyema, anti-TB therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: When fibrothorax is the result of an active ongoing infection — empyema or tuberculous pleurisy — treating the underlying infection with appropriate antibiotics or anti-tuberculosis quadruple therapy is essential to halt ongoing pleural injury and fibrin deposition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate antibiotics for bacterial empyema and standard 6-month anti-TB therapy for tuberculous pleurisy clear the active infection in more than 90% of cases when started before extensive fibrosis has formed.

surgicalOptions.name: VATS decortication (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery)
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive removal of the fibrous pleural cortex using thoracoscopic instruments through 2-3 small incisions; preferred for softer, less calcified cortex in early fibrothorax. Shorter hospital stay (3-5 days) and faster recovery compared with open surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful lung re-expansion achieved in approximately 70-85% of appropriately selected cases; conversion to open thoracotomy required in 15-30% of VATS decortications due to cortex density.

surgicalOptions.name: Open decortication (Fowler-Stephenson procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: Full thoracotomy with surgical peeling of the fibrous peel from the visceral and parietal pleural surfaces; required for calcified, dense, or bilateral fibrothorax and for cases where VATS cannot achieve complete lung expansion. The definitive operation for advanced fibrothorax.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Lung re-expansion rates of 75-90% in series of selected patients with viable underlying lung; mortality risk of 1-5% in specialised thoracic centres, higher in patients with poor pre-operative lung function.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured exercise training combined with breathing techniques and education; improves exercise tolerance and dyspnoea in patients too unwell for surgery or as adjunctive care after decortication.

alternativeTreatments.name: Chest physiotherapy and postural drainage
alternativeTreatments.description: Helps maintain airway clearance from the atelectatic lung, reduces infection risk, and preserves residual chest wall mobility in patients managed conservatively.

alternativeTreatments.name: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV)
alternativeTreatments.description: Bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) ventilation may relieve dyspnoea and correct nocturnal hypoventilation in patients with severe bilateral fibrothorax who are not surgical candidates; evidence in fibrothorax specifically is limited but extrapolated from restrictive lung disease management.

whySeeSpecialist: A thoracic surgeon or respiratory physician with pleural disease expertise should be consulted for any patient with progressive breathlessness, restrictive spirometry, and persistent pleural opacity following haemothorax, empyema, or tuberculosis. Thoracic surgeons assess surgical candidacy for decortication and determine whether VATS or open thoracotomy is appropriate. Pulmonologists manage the medical aspects and investigate ambiguous cases where mesothelioma must be excluded. Early specialist referral — before the cortex calcifies — preserves the opportunity for less invasive VATS rather than open surgery.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a thoracic surgeon with specific experience in video-assisted and open decortication, performing at least 20-30 decortications per year in a dedicated thoracic centre. The surgical team should have access to advanced pleural imaging (HRCT and nuclear split-function scanning), intraoperative bronchoscopy, and intensive care facilities for post-operative management. Respiratory physicians with pleural disease subspecialty (ideally with experience in the British Thoracic Society or American Thoracic Society pleural disease guidelines) provide the diagnostic and pre-operative workup.
preventionStrategies: Drain all clinically significant haemothoraces promptly — VATS drainage within 3-5 days of a retained haemothorax prevents clot organisation and dramatically reduces fibrothorax risk.
Treat pleural empyema aggressively at the fibrinopurulent stage with intrapleural fibrinolytics or surgical drainage before the organising stage begins.
Complete a full course of treatment for tuberculosis pleurisy to minimise the pleural fibrosing reaction.
Minimise asbestos exposure in occupational settings and use respiratory protection when working with asbestos-containing materials.
Report progressive breathlessness after any previous pleural illness promptly so that early fibrosis can be identified and treated before it calcifies.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid smoking, which impairs mucociliary clearance and increases infection risk in the atelectatic lung, potentially accelerating fibrothorax complications.
Participate in supervised pulmonary rehabilitation to maintain exercise capacity and breathing efficiency.
Receive annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination to reduce the risk of chest infections that can precipitate acute deterioration.
Report any new or worsening chest symptoms promptly, particularly fever or increased sputum, which may indicate infection in the atelectatic lung.
If prescribed supplemental oxygen, use it for the recommended number of hours daily to maintain adequate blood oxygen levels.
Maintain a healthy weight — obesity worsens the restrictive physiology of fibrothorax by adding external mechanical load to already compromised lungs.

recommended: Adequate protein and caloric intake to support respiratory muscle strength and post-surgical healing
Plenty of fluids to maintain airway hydration and facilitate mucus clearance
Antioxidant-rich foods (fruit, vegetables, whole grains) to support immune function and reduce oxidative stress
Small frequent meals rather than large meals, which are better tolerated when respiratory reserve is limited
avoid: Smoking and all tobacco products — paramount in anyone with compromised lung function
Large heavy meals that push the diaphragm up and reduce functional residual capacity in those with severe restriction
Excess alcohol, which suppresses respiratory drive and increases infection susceptibility

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is strongly encouraged in all patients with fibrothorax who are medically stable. Regular aerobic activity — beginning at whatever level is comfortable and gradually increasing — preserves diaphragmatic strength, improves cardiac output compensation, and slows the deconditioning cycle that accelerates disability. Patients with severe restriction (VC below 50% predicted) or resting hypoxaemia should be assessed by a pulmonary rehabilitation team before starting an independent exercise programme, and supplemental oxygen during exercise may be required. After decortication, a six-to-twelve week graduated return to activity is guided by the surgical team, with physiotherapy-supervised breathing exercises from day one post-operatively.
dailyManagement: Practise prescribed breathing exercises daily — incentive spirometry and active cycle of breathing techniques maintain chest wall mobility.
Monitor breathlessness on a simple daily scale and contact your medical team if it worsens over consecutive days without a clear trigger.
Take prescribed medications consistently — oxygen, bronchodilators, and any anti-infective agents should not be stopped without medical review.
Avoid respiratory irritants including dust, chemical fumes, and second-hand smoke, all of which increase airway secretions and risk of infection in the entrapped lung.
Attend all scheduled spirometry and imaging follow-up appointments so that any progression can be identified early.
prognosis: The prognosis of fibrothorax depends heavily on whether the underlying lung remains viable and whether surgical decortication can be performed before the cortex calcifies. In patients who undergo successful decortication with full lung re-expansion, vital capacity improvement of 15-30% has been reported in series, with meaningful relief of dyspnoea in the majority and a return to normal or near-normal activities in younger patients with no underlying parenchymal disease. The improvement continues for six to twelve months after surgery as the lung gradually re-expands and the chest wall remodels. Patients who are not surgical candidates, or who have bilateral fibrothorax with severely compromised underlying parenchyma, face a progressive restrictive course with declining exercise tolerance and, eventually, respiratory failure and cor pulmonale. In this group, management is palliative, and prognosis is comparable to end-stage restrictive lung disease from other causes. Fibrothorax from tuberculosis carries a somewhat worse prognosis than post-empyema disease in the same grade, because the cortex tends to calcify earlier, reducing the window for surgical benefit. Asbestos-related fibrothorax requires vigilance for the development of mesothelioma, which can complicate the picture and significantly worsens overall outlook.
recoveryTimeline: After successful surgical decortication, the initial hospital stay is typically 5-10 days, with chest drains in place until air leaks stop and fluid drainage is minimal. Most patients can walk comfortably on the ward within two to three days of surgery. Full lung re-expansion on imaging takes six to twelve weeks, and the improvement in vital capacity continues progressively over this period. Patients typically return to light daily activities within four to six weeks and full activity within three to four months if no complications arise. Post-operative physiotherapy spanning eight to twelve weeks is an important component of recovery. Conservative (non-surgical) management of mild fibrothorax does not produce improvement in lung volumes but can stabilise symptoms with appropriate pulmonary rehabilitation over months.
complications: Respiratory failure requiring supplemental oxygen or ventilatory support in patients with severe bilateral fibrothorax, arising when the combined restrictive defect reduces effective alveolar ventilation below the threshold for adequate gas exchange.

Cor pulmonale and right heart failure secondary to chronic hypoxaemia driving pulmonary vasoconstriction, signalled by ankle oedema, elevated jugular venous pressure, and right ventricular enlargement on echocardiography.

Superimposed pleural empyema or pneumonia within the atelectatic entrapped lung, which is prone to repeated infection from retained secretions, sometimes presenting with fever and pleuritic pain years after the original fibrothorax formed.

Mesothelioma complicating asbestos-related fibrothorax — malignant transformation of the pleural mesothelium is a life-threatening late complication in asbestos-exposed individuals that must be distinguished from benign progressive fibrothorax.
Scoliosis and chest wall deformity from longstanding unilateral fibrothorax, particularly in children in whom the rigid pleural cortex prevents normal chest cage development.

Post-decortication complications including prolonged air leak, haemothorax, re-expansion pulmonary oedema, and, rarely, bronchopleural fistula, each requiring specific management in the post-operative period.

supportResources.name: British Thoracic Society — Pleural Disease Guidelines
supportResources.description: UK national guidelines on management of pleural effusion, empyema, and related pleural conditions, including prevention and early treatment of empyema to avoid fibrothorax.

supportResources.name: American Thoracic Society — Patient Information: Pleural Diseases
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resource from the American Thoracic Society covering pleural conditions including empyema and its complications.

supportResources.name: NHS — Empyema (pleural empyema) information
supportResources.description: NHS patient page on pleural empyema, the most common precursor of fibrothorax, covering symptoms, treatment, and when to seek urgent care.

sources.title: Davies HE et al. Effect of an indwelling pleural catheter vs chest tube and talc pleurodesis for recurrent malignant pleural effusion. JAMA 2012
Rahman NM et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection (MIST2 trial). NEJM 2011
Heffner JE. Multicenter trials of treatment strategies for empyema. Clin Infect Dis 2010
Lim TK. Fibrothorax and decortication. Eur Respir J 2002
American Thoracic Society — Diagnosis and management of malignant mesothelioma. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2018

confusedWithConditions.name: Malignant pleural effusion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malignant pleural effusion from mesothelioma or metastatic cancer can produce progressive pleural thickening that mimics fibrothorax on imaging; cytology, biopsy, and the clinical context of prior malignancy distinguish the two — mesothelioma requires urgent oncological management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pleural effusion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An unorganised pleural effusion is free fluid in the pleural space that can be drained; fibrothorax is solid fibrous tissue that requires surgery — the distinction is made by CT showing enhancement, septations, and solid cortex rather than fluid attenuation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumothorax
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumothorax is air in the pleural space producing hyperresonance and absent breath sounds on the affected side; fibrothorax produces dullness and reduced expansion. Chest X-ray clearly differentiates the two, and confusion is rare except in complex post-operative cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pleurisy (pleuritis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pleurisy is active pleural inflammation causing sharp pleuritic chest pain and a friction rub; fibrothorax is the late sequela of resolved pleural disease characterised by dull ache, breathlessness, and imaging evidence of pleural thickening rather than active inflammation.

relatedConditions.name: Hemothorax
relatedConditions.relevance: Undrained haemothorax is the most common traumatic cause of fibrothorax; the transition from liquid blood to clotted haemothorax to organised fibrothorax occurs over days to weeks without timely drainage.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumothorax
relatedConditions.relevance: Pneumothorax can co-occur with haemothorax (haemopneumothorax) and, when inadequately drained, contributes to the pleural scarring that leads to fibrothorax.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tuberculous pleurisy is a leading infectious cause of fibrothorax globally and characteristically produces the calcified bilateral pleural thickening seen in chronic disease.

relatedConditions.name: Mesothelioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Asbestos-related fibrothorax increases the clinical and radiological overlap with mesothelioma; distinguishing benign fibrothorax from early mesothelioma requires histology.

faqs.question: What is fibrothorax?
faqs.answer: Fibrothorax is a condition where thick scar tissue forms in the pleural space, fusing the two layers of the pleural membrane and preventing the lung from fully expanding. It usually develops after an undrained haemothorax or pleural empyema.

faqs.question: What causes fibrothorax?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are an incompletely drained haemothorax (blood in the chest from trauma or surgery) and pleural empyema (infected fluid from pneumonia). Tuberculosis, asbestos exposure, and chest radiotherapy are other important causes.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of fibrothorax?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is progressive breathlessness on exertion. Other symptoms include reduced exercise tolerance, a dull chest ache on the affected side, visible chest asymmetry, and recurrent chest infections from the poorly-ventilated trapped lung.

faqs.question: How is fibrothorax diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a history of prior pleural disease, a chest X-ray showing a dense pleural opacity with reduced hemithoracic volume, and high-resolution CT confirming cortex thickness. Pulmonary function tests show a restrictive pattern with reduced vital capacity.

faqs.question: Can fibrothorax be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Mild fibrothorax with minimal symptoms can be managed conservatively with pulmonary rehabilitation and monitoring. However, for significant breathlessness and proven viable underlying lung, surgical decortication is the only intervention that restores lung expansion.

faqs.question: What is decortication surgery for fibrothorax?
faqs.answer: Decortication involves surgically removing the thick fibrous peel from the surface of the lung and chest wall to allow the lung to re-expand. It can be done with minimally invasive thoracoscopic surgery or, for dense calcified cortex, through an open thoracotomy incision.

faqs.question: What is the recovery time after decortication?
faqs.answer: Hospital stay is typically 5-10 days. Most patients return to light activities within four to six weeks and full activity within three to four months. Lung volumes continue improving for up to 12 months as the freed lung gradually re-expands.

faqs.question: Can fibrothorax be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, largely. Prompt and complete drainage of haemothorax within 3-5 days and early surgical or fibrinolytic treatment of empyema before the fibrinopurulent stage advances are the most effective preventive measures.

faqs.question: Does fibrothorax cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: If decortication is performed before the underlying lung parenchyma is permanently damaged, significant recovery is possible. If the lung has fibrosed or the cortex has calcified without treatment, the restriction becomes irreversible even after surgery.

faqs.question: How thick does the pleural cortex need to be before surgery is needed?
faqs.answer: A pleural cortex of 2 cm or more covering more than half the pleural surface, combined with significant breathlessness and proven expandable underlying lung on nuclear scan, is the generally accepted threshold for surgical decortication.

faqs.question: Is fibrothorax the same as pleural effusion?
faqs.answer: No. A pleural effusion is free fluid in the pleural space that can be drained with a needle or drain. Fibrothorax is solid organised scar tissue that requires surgery. Fibrothorax often develops from a pleural effusion that was not drained or not drained completely.

faqs.question: Can tuberculosis cause fibrothorax?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tuberculous pleurisy can produce a particularly dense fibrosing reaction with calcified pleural plaques. It is a leading cause of fibrothorax in countries where tuberculosis is common, including parts of Africa, Asia, and Eastern Europe.

faqs.question: Does asbestos exposure cause fibrothorax?
faqs.answer: Prolonged asbestos exposure causes diffuse pleural thickening and hyaline pleural plaques that can progress to bilateral fibrothorax encasing both lungs. The onset is typically 20-40 years after first exposure, and affected individuals need surveillance for mesothelioma.

faqs.question: What does fibrothorax look like on a chest X-ray?
faqs.answer: On X-ray, fibrothorax appears as a dense white opacity over one hemithorax with reduced lung volume on that side, often with mediastinal shift toward the affected side. Calcified plaques appear as irregular white densities over the pleural surface.

faqs.question: Can fibrothorax affect both sides of the chest?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral fibrothorax occurs, most commonly from tuberculosis or asbestos exposure. It causes more severe restriction than unilateral disease and is associated with a higher risk of respiratory failure. Bilateral disease is more challenging to treat surgically.

faqs.question: What happens if fibrothorax is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, progressive restriction continues as the cortex thickens and calcifies. Eventually respiratory failure, pulmonary hypertension, and cor pulmonale develop. Recurrent infections in the trapped lung add to the burden. Early treatment gives dramatically better outcomes.

faqs.question: Why do I keep getting chest infections after a pleural empyema?
faqs.answer: If empyema was not fully drained, the resulting fibrothorax traps part of the lung in a poorly ventilated state. Retained secretions in the atelectatic segment promote bacterial growth. This is why recurrent infections prompt investigation for underlying fibrothorax.

faqs.question: Can fibrothorax be confused with lung cancer?
faqs.answer: On imaging, fibrothorax and mesothelioma (a pleural cancer linked to asbestos) can look similar. When the cause is unclear or there is a history of asbestos exposure, pleural biopsy is needed to exclude malignancy before labelling the thickening as benign fibrothorax.

faqs.question: Is fibrothorax painful?
faqs.answer: Established fibrothorax typically causes a dull ache rather than sharp pain. Pleuritic pain during breathing is more characteristic of the active inflammation that precedes fibrothorax. Acute pain during or after decortication surgery is managed with epidural or regional anaesthetic techniques.

faqs.question: How long does it take for fibrothorax to develop after haemothorax?
faqs.answer: Fibrin deposition begins within 24-48 hours of blood entering the pleural space. Organised fibrous cortex is usually established by four to twelve weeks if the haemothorax is not drained. This is why VATS drainage within 3-5 days of a retained haemothorax is the recommended window for prevention.

metaDescription: Fibrothorax is pleural scarring that traps the lung, causing progressive breathlessness. Learn causes, diagnosis, decortication surgery and prognosis.
